description: Something is 'sukaro' when its temperature is high and the heat plainly felt. A hot pan can burn unprotected skin, while hot weather limits how long a person can remain outside. 'Sulae' (warm) covers moderate heat; 'suloru' (fever) names a bodily condition rather than temperature alone.
articulatory_notes: A narrow groove along the tongue directs voiceless 's' into rounded high-back 'u'. The back of the tongue then closes against the soft palate for 'k' and releases into open central 'a'. A tap or short trill begins the final syllable before rounded mid-back 'o'. Stress falls on 'ka'.
sound_symbolism: The opening 'su' is shared with 'sulae' (warm) and 'suloru' (fever), an audible kinship rather than a productive affix. The closure of 'k' makes stressed open 'a' arrive abruptly, unlike the flowing hiatus of 'sulae'.
usage_notes: The form 'sukaro phialu' is hot water; 'sukaro howenu' is hot weather. Exact temperatures and thresholds stay with the measurement or source record when they matter.

semantic_domains.nature: weather or environmental heat that is strongly felt
semantic_domains.physical: high temperature strongly felt through contact or surrounding air
